narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid but challenging background, we've carefully reviewed your law school application through our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on academic and personal factors. Your profile includes Year 1 grades placing you in the third decile at your school, Year 3 grades in the first decile, a lower-middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 31, an undergraduate GPA of 2.30, and plans to attend part-time while working. The model estimates a 48% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect both you and the school's outcomes. The primary reason for this decision was your Year 3 academic decile, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure—it's notably lower than what the model sees in most successful students. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success compared to higher-income peers who sometimes face different pressures. Your LSAT score and Year 1 decile also helped somewhat, as they suggested potential that aligned better with passers, though not enough to offset the top concern. Being in the part-time track nudged things slightly toward failure, since full-time students (who make up over 90% of the dataset) tend to pass at higher rates. Overall, your Year 3 performance was well below the typical fifth-decile average for students who pass, making it the dominant influence here. While your undergraduate GPA offered a small boost, it was minor compared to these others. This isn't a reflection of your worth or potential as a white male from a modest background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or retakes, consider full-time options if feasible, and retake the LSAT to push above the average of 36.7. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these areas, and our admissions team is here to discuss personalized strategies. Keep pursuing your goals—you've got this.
